did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a hybrid deep learning model named SkinCaNet, which combines Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Transformer architectures (specifically Feed Forward Network blocks, Multi-Head Self-Attention blocks, and an MLP head), to classify dermoscopic images of Melanoma and Nonmelanoma skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a novel deep learning model named SkinCaNet for classifying skin lesions. It details the methodology, dataset (HAM10000), training parameters, and performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, specificity) achieved by the proposed model, and compares these results with state-of-the-art models. Although published in a conference proceedings (ICBSP 2023), it contains original research results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: An automated skin lesions classification using hybrid CNN and transformer based deep learning model
journal: 2023 8th International Conference on Biomedical Imaging, Signal Processing (ICBSP 2023)
brief_summary: This study proposes SkinCaNet, a hybrid deep learning model combining Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Transformer architectures to classify dermoscopic images of Melanoma and Nonmelanoma skin lesions. The model was trained from scratch on the HAM10000 dataset, utilizing data augmentation to address class imbalance and a convolution stem for input embedding. Evaluation on the test set demonstrated that SkinCaNet achieved an accuracy of 92.38%, with a precision of 95.62%, recall of 82.92%, and specificity of 97.82%. Comparative analysis showed that SkinCaNet outperformed state-of-the-art models such as MobileNetV3, EfficientNetV2, and GhostNet in terms of accuracy and error rate. The results indicate that integrating local feature extraction via CNN with global dependency modeling via self-attention mechanisms enhances classification performance for skin cancer diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two binary classes used in the HAM10000 dataset for classification. The dataset contained 3424 images of Melanoma.

One of the two binary classes used in the HAM10000 dataset for classification. The dataset contained 5955 images of Nonmelanoma. The paper notes that Nonmelanoma accounts for the vast majority of everyday moles and explicitly links this class to 'nevus' in the context of previous works (Damian et al. and Zhou et al.) and in the abstract.

all_task_types.details: Binary classification task distinguishing between Melanoma and Nonmelanoma skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (Melanoma vs. Nonmelanoma) using a sigmoid classifier in the MLP head. Performance metrics include accuracy (92.38%), precision (95.62%), recall (82.92%), and specificity (97.82%).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset of dermoscopic images from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) annual challenge, specifically the ISIC 2018 dataset commonly known as HAM10000. It contains imbalanced class distribution between Melanoma and Nonmelanoma (Nevus).

use.training: Used for training the SkinCaNet model from scratch. The training set consisted of 7503 images (2739 Melanoma, 4764 Nonmelanoma). Data augmentation (rotation, shifting, shear, flip, zoom) was applied to the training data to address class imbalance and prevent overfitting.
use.testing: Used for evaluating the model's performance. The test set consisted of 1876 images (685 Melanoma, 1191 Nonmelanoma). Performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, specificity, error rate) and confusion matrix were calculated on this set.
use.other: Used for comparative analysis against state-of-the-art models (MobileNetV3, EfficientNetV2, GhostNet, MobileVitV2) trained with the same hyperparameters.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, specificity, error rate) without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.notes: SkinCaNet outperformed all compared SOTA models in accuracy and error rate. SkinCaNet performed equally well on both Melanoma and Nonmelanoma classes, whereas CNN models were better on Melanoma and transformer models were better on Nonmelanoma.

architecture_details.ffn_details: Combines depth-wise 3x3 convolution (DWCONV) and FFN; uses learnable layer scale and per-block expansion ratio for flexible computation
architecture_details.mhss_details: DWCONV adds local information to Value matrix; fully connected layers added to dimensions of heads; stride attention technique employed where Q, K, V are downsampled to spatial resolution of 32 and interpolated back; Queries downsampled via pooling (static local downsampling) and DWCONV (learnable local downsampling)
architecture_details.classifier_head: MLP head comprising dense layers and Swish activation function

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing all images to a uniform size of 224 × 224

Data augmentation to address class imbalance, specifically geometric augmentation including: rotation by 50 degrees, vertical and horizontal shifting by 0.2, shear transformation, horizontal flip, and zoom by a factor of 0.2

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to training data to address class imbalance. Specific transforms included: rotation by 50 degrees, vertical and horizontal shifting by 0.2, shear transformation, horizontal flip, and zoom by a factor of 0.2.

conclusions: The proposed SkinCaNet model, a hybrid deep learning architecture combining Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Transformer mechanisms, achieved an accuracy of 92.38%, precision of 95.62%, recall of 82.92%, and specificity of 97.82% in classifying Melanoma and Nonmelanoma dermoscopic images.

SkinCaNet demonstrated superior performance compared to state-of-the-art models (MobileNetV3, EfficientNetV2, GhostNet, MobileViTV2) and previous literature works (Damian et al., Zhou et al.) in terms of accuracy and error rate.

The integration of local feature extraction via CNN (Feed Forward Network blocks) with global dependency modeling via self-attention (Multi-Head Self-Attention blocks) enhances classification performance, allowing the model to capture both local and global features effectively.

The hybrid architecture proved more robust than using CNN or Transformer architectures alone, particularly in dealing with the imbalanced dataset distribution between Melanoma and Nonmelanoma classes.

SkinCaNet performed equally well on both Melanoma and Nonmelanoma classes, whereas CNN models were generally better on Melanoma and Transformer models were better on Nonmelanoma in comparative contexts.
The use of a convolution stem for input embedding and an MLP head with Swish activation function contributed to improved optimization and classification accuracy.

The authors acknowledge limitations regarding the number of training parameters and plan future work to reduce parameters while maintaining accuracy, enhance the model for image segmentation to detect abnormalities, and collect more data to verify performance.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a binary classification task (Melanoma vs. Nonmelanoma/Nevus), ignoring other clinically significant skin lesion types present in the HAM10000 dataset (e.g., BCC, AK, DF, IPL, VASC).

The model was trained from scratch on the HAM10000 dataset without using transfer learning, which is a common baseline in this domain, potentially limiting the generalizability of the 'superiority' claims compared to models leveraging pre-trained weights.

The paper acknowledges a limitation regarding the high number of training parameters and does not report the parameter count or computational complexity (FLOPs), which is a significant concern for deploying hybrid CNN-Transformer models in clinical settings.

The dataset used (HAM10000) has a known imbalanced class distribution (3424 Melanoma vs. 5955 Nonmelanoma); while data augmentation was applied, the paper does not report stratified cross-validation results, relying instead on a single 80/20 train-test split which may introduce variance.
No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on the HAM10000 test set, raising concerns about generalizability to other populations or dermoscopic devices.

The paper does not report any statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for the performance differences between SkinCaNet and the compared SOTA models (MobileNetV3, EfficientNetV2, etc.), making it unclear if the observed improvements are statistically significant.

The authors note that CNN models alone were better at Melanoma detection while Transformer models were better at Nonmelanoma detection, suggesting that the hybrid architecture's balance is a key contribution, but the specific contribution of each component (CNN vs. Transformer) to the final accuracy is not ablated in the provided text.

The study was published in a conference proceeding (ICBSP 2023) with only 6 pages, which may limit the depth of methodological detail and ablation studies typically expected in peer-reviewed journal articles.
